title: School Phone Ban
description: A school board is debating whether phones should be banned during the school day.

participants.name: Dana
participants.statement: A phone ban would help students focus and improve learning.

participants.name: Noah
participants.statement: Students need phones for safety and family emergencies.

participants.name: Mina
participants.statement: Teachers need a clear policy because enforcement is inconsistent.

participants.name: Eli
participants.statement: The school should not punish students who need accessibility tools.

participants.name: Fern
participants.statement: Parents do not trust the district to handle emergencies well.

participants.name: Theo
participants.statement: Maybe phones can be stored during class but available during lunch.

participants.name: Ms. Okonkwo
participants.statement: I've taught middle school for 22 years. Phones in pockets aren't the problem — adults handing them to 11-year-olds without limits is. A school ban without a parent-side conversation just shifts the conflict.

participants.name: Yuki
participants.statement: I'm a Type 1 diabetic. My continuous glucose monitor sends alerts to my phone. A blanket ban would put my health at risk. Carve-outs need to be specific, not optional.

participants.name: Marcus
participants.statement: I'm a senior. My family doesn't have wi-fi at home. I do half my homework on my phone using mobile data during free periods. Banning it just means I fall behind.

participants.name: Sister Ana
participants.statement: I work in a parish after-school program. The bigger issue is what kids do with phones at home — porn, bullying, sleep loss. School is a small slice of the day.

participants.name: Reza
participants.statement: I'm a working dad with two kids in middle school. I need to text my daughter about pickup changes. A ban is fine if there's a school-approved messaging system that actually works.

participants.name: Jordan
participants.statement: I'm a queer freshman. My phone is how I stay in touch with my support community. Some kids' phones are a lifeline, not a distraction.

participants.name: Mr. Bradshaw
participants.statement: I'm the school resource officer. I can tell you a lockdown drill works better when students aren't filming or pranking. There's a real safety reason to limit phones during structured time.
